content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

April 3, 1997

John L. Fellows III
City Attorney
City of Torrance
3031 Torrance Boulevard
Torrance, California 90503

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-97-132

Dear Mr. Fellows:

This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Because you have not disclosed the identity of the person or
persons for whom you seek this advice, we are treating this as a request for informal assistance,
as required under Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed). Please bear in mind that nothing in this
letter should be construed as evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place.
Further, this letter is based on the facts as they have been presented to us. The Commission does
not act as finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May a candidate who was elected to the Torrance City Council in the March 5, 1996,
election receive contributions at this time to retire campaign debt from that election?

CONCLUSION

No. That portion of the Torrance Municipal Code limiting post-election fundraising
permits no post-election fundraising after June 30, 1996, even for purposes of debt retirement.
Proposition 208 contains such an exception in Section 85305(c), but Proposition 208 does not
preempt a local law that is at least as stringent as parallel portions of Proposition 208.

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18895, of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-132
Page 2

FACTS

A provision of the Torrance Municipal Code ("TMC"), which was not enacted by a vote
of the people, limits the time during which candidates in municipal elections may receive
campaign contributions. The period within which any contributions might be received begins
with the candidate's filing of a statement of intention to run, and ends on June 30th following the
city election for which the statement of intention was filed. Municipal elections are typically
held in early March. The last election for city council was held on March 5, 1996. Thus, under
the TMC, a candidate was not permitted to raise funds after June 30, 1996, for a campaign
leading up to the municipal election the previous March. This is true even if the purpose of such
fundraising were to retire campaign debt incurred prior to the election, since the TMC makes no
exception to its post-election fundraising provision for debt retirement.

ANALYSIS

Proposition 208, approved by the voters on November 5, 1996, became effective by its
terms on January 1, 1997. Proposition 208 introduces into the Political Reform Act a variety of
provisions governing the amount, timing, and permissible sources of campaign contributions.
Proposition 208 recognized that many local jurisdictions already had similar contribution
limitations, or might wish to enact such limitations in the future, and anticipated that "conflict of
law" questions might arise in such cases. Thus Section 85706 provides in pertinent part:

"(a) Nothing in this act shall nullify contribution limitations or
other campaign disclosures or prohibitions of any local jurisdiction
that are as or more stringent than set forth in this act.
(b) The governing body of a local jurisdiction may impose lower
contribution limitations or other campaign disclosures or
prohibitions that are as or more stringent than set forth in this act.
A local jurisdiction may impose higher contribution or expenditure
limitations only by a vote of the people."

* * *

In essence, Section 85706 permits the governing body of any local jurisdiction to impose
its own contribution limits or other campaign disclosure provisions and prohibitions, so long as
those provisions or prohibitions are "as or more stringent" than those mandated by Proposition
208. Section 85706(b) permits a local jurisdiction to enact contribution or expenditure limits
higher than those of Proposition 208, but only by a vote of the people.² It is important to note
that the permissible scope of the voters' action under subsection (b) is restricted to enactment of
"contribution or expenditure limitations" higher than those of Proposition 208. Even the voters

²    We have interpreted this language to include contribution or expenditure limits already in place when
Proposition 208 became effective. (Mueller Advice Letter, No. A-96-353.)

File No. A-97-132
Page 3

in local jurisdictions are not permitted to enact "campaign disclosures or other prohibitions" less
stringent than those of Proposition 208.³

With these "conflict of law" rules in mind, we turn to Section 85305(c), the provision of
Proposition 208 that governs the issues you present. Section 85305(c) states, in full:

"(c) No candidate or the controlled committee of such candidate shall
accept contributions more than 90 days after the date of withdrawal,
defeat, or election to office. Contributions accepted immediately
following such an election or withdrawal and up to 90 days after that date
shall be used only to pay outstanding bills or debts owed by the candidate
or controlled committee. This section shall not apply to retiring debts
incurred with respect to any election held prior to the effective date of this
act, provided such funds are collected pursuant to the contribution limits
specified in Article 3 (commencing with Section 85300) of this act,
applied separately for each prior election for which debts are being retired,
and such funds raised shall not count against the contribution limitations
applicable for any election following the effective date of this act."

Insofar as it permits fundraising until June 30 following early March elections, the TMC
limitation on post-election fundraising is "less stringent" than Proposition 208's corresponding
limitation in the first sentence of Section 85305(c), which provides that fundraising cease within
90 days after the election. Taking the 1996 Torrance municipal election as an example, the
permissible fundraising period following the March 5 election was 116 days, from March 6
through June 30. If Proposition 208 had been in effect at the time, its 90 day post-election
fundraising limit would have run 26 days earlier. The fundraising window imposed by
Proposition 208 is plainly "more stringent" (because it is shorter) and preempts that portion of
the TMC that would allow for a more lengthy post-election fundraising period.⁴

³ Section 81013 provides much the same, as follows: "Nothing in this title prevents the Legislature or any
other state or local agency from imposing additional requirements on any person if the requirements do not prevent
the person from complying with this title. If any act of the Legislature conflicts with the provisions of this
title shall prevail."

⁴ For a fuller discussion of Proposition 208's preemptive effects, see the Carnavale Advice Letter, No. A-
97-104 (copy enclosed). Carnavale examined Proposition B, a Los Angeles measure approved by the voters in the
same election that saw the passage of Proposition 208. Proposition B provided a shorter off-year fundraising ban
than did Proposition 208, but we concluded that that local provision amounted to a higher contribution limitation
which, since it was passed by the voters, was not preempted by Proposition 208. (Section 85706(b).) We also
determined that the debt reduction provisions of Section 85305(c) applied to Los Angeles County candidates
retiring debt from elections held prior to the effective date of Proposition B, which was silent on contribution limits
applicable to post-election debt reduction. We concluded that where the local ordinance was silent on such an issue,
Proposition 208 would fill any gaps. As shown in the following discussion, the TMC contrasts with Proposition B
in prohibiting all fundraising after a certain date following the election. As a result, there are no "gaps" to fill, and
preemption is not required since the local ordinance is "as or more stringent" than Proposition 208.

File No. A-97-132
Page 4

Although Proposition 208 mandates a shorter period overall for post-election fundraising,
the third sentence of Section 85305(c) expressly exempts from this limitation post-election
fundraising for "retiring debts incurred with respect to any election held prior to the effective
date of this act," i.e., January 1, 1997. Thus, under an uncomplicated application of Section
85305(c), a candidate in the 1996 Torrance municipal election would, beginning on January 1,
1997, be able to raise funds to retire any debt incurred "with respect to" that election.

But Section 85305(c) cannot be applied in this simple manner, in the face of a local
statute that addresses post-election fundraising with a blanket prohibition admitting of no
exceptions like those contained in Section 85305(c). Although Proposition 208 preempts that
part of the TMC that would enlarge Proposition 208's 90 day post-election fundraising cutoff, it
does not preempt any portion of the TMC that is more stringent than the provisions of
Proposition 208. The TMC's failure to grant any exception permitting continued post-election
debt retirement fundraising is clearly "more stringent" than Proposition 208, which does grant
such an exception. Section 85706(a), quoted above, demonstrates an unequivocal intent that
Proposition 208 not be applied to preempt any local measure that imposes more stringent
fundraising restrictions than those established by Proposition 208.

The outcome of this analysis may seem harsh in both shortening the post-election
fundraising window previously available under the TMC, and at the same time giving continued
effect to the TMC's more restrictive treatment of post-election debt retirement. It is, however,
fully consistent with both the letter and the spirit of Proposition 208. The alternative would be to
apply Proposition 208 to reverse a local prohibition that had entirely shut down fundraising for
1996 elections, by reopening the fundraising window on January 1, 1997, when Proposition 208
became effective. Such a result is plainly contrary to the intent expressed in Section 85706(a).

If you have any other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

[signature: Laurence T. Woodlock]

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:LTW:ak
